Practical research exists toward known application - mostly to improve a process or solve bottlenecks. The goal is to reduce cognitive cost by managing complexity. Theoretical research on the other hand advances the discipline without any specific practical goals. The endpoint emerges from constraints in the theorization itself - the goal is in the act.

But 'memetics research' sits in a weird place...  its practical aspect is destabilizing the layer at which a thought is being held. But how do you research what to destabilize without falling into the same trap yourself? like, you you can't step outside the medium to examine the medium. you're still going to be using pattern completion to examine pattern completion. the frame you're trying to destabilize is the frame you're thinking through. this is what i'm sitting with at this moment... anyone have a light to shed on this for me?

<@458345600166002700> have you thought about "when exactly does an A become a B" ?
like is the answer obvious / what's your reaction to this question

ok so, I have an idea for a project, but it would need someone with better crypto/blockchain chops than I have to actually build it, and it's not cryptocurrency based so it's not a "get rich quick" opportunity.
It's just a technology that obviously needs to exist for *actual* existential risk reasons
It's also *incredibly* simple and straightforward and obvious, so much so that  I've been talking about it for about 10 years (with no traction. for some stupid reason)

basically, we need a public blockchain and a cryptographically secure id chip in all digital cameras, such that a photograph can be "proven" genuine, but only by the owner of the original camera that took the picture

the process is pretty straightforward: a picture is taken, and then a cryptographic hash of its date/time, location, and contents is uploaded to a blockchain. The owner of that camera can later prove that their camera generated that hash, and that it corresponds to an image produced at the claimed date/time with that physical device
this solves all kinds of "deepfakes", AI or otherwise
it feels obvious, or like, "meh"
like yeah, it's an A at first, you're all excited, then it "integrates down" to a B and you can now build from there
so I guess plausibly something like "once it stops being your cutting edge" or something, but this doesn't feel super profound/meaningful to think about 👀

wrote this in my journal today

.

I wanted to understand the world i found myself in
so I started telling myself stories. every day I would generate a virtual reality and say "this is the world", and I would go out into the world and use this virtual reality as my minimap

over time some stories stuck with me while others faded from memory. some stories were interesting. some made me laugh some made me cry. some made me depressed. some made me feel like a god. some would give me new abilities, like the ability to regulate my emotions a bit better. some would make me more ambitious and some less so. some would spread to others and be repeated. some would be larger and deeper in scope.at some point when stories started sticking around for longer and becoming more grand. I wondered, what is the most grand story I could tell myself?and so I started telling myself the grandest story I could make, working on it bit by bit every day

even asking what the grandest story is forced me to reconsider everything I know, I had to go back to the drawing board. where does the story start? why there and not somewhere else? what is the range of possible ways to start a story? what should the central theme of the story be? what should the story be about? 

so I started telling stories, specifically optimized for giving me phenomenal experience of "oh this feels fundamental, this is the core of the matter"

.

one of the first stories that made feel this is one reminiscent of the tree of life. where the patterns of the abstract are manifested physically in the world. as they are in genes and in brains. where knowledge is the physical manifestation of abstractions. a marriage between the two sperate realms of the physical and abstract. the core was to be about knowledge, so epistemology, and further not just any knowledge, but moral knowledge, since it is stories about what informs my action, that made me have the phenomenal experience of being fundamental. since knowing something about how abstractions become manifest implies something about how it should be, it implies something about the physical systems that instantiate knowledge, such as agents, and it implies something about how they should behave. quite simply, if knowledge is to be a fundamental part of the story then knowledge bearing systems should be too

this idea wasn't abandoned but something that felt more fundamental came along. the framing I ran into on some twitter account's substack, framed being and becoming, as two fundamental categories, and it places religions/cultures into each bucket, generally Eastern as being and western as becoming. this framing struck me as profound. and it stuck around. and I found myself reaching for it more and more often when telling stories.

.

it was then when a man called luv contacted me and told me his story of his meaning crisis, where he compressed the most grand story he could tell himself and told it to me. he has cancer and is likely going to die. his story is about his accelerated trek through local minima until he hit what he considers to be a global minimum, which resolved his meaning crisis in a way where he claimed it is impossible for him to ever have a meaning crisis ever again. his story centered around love and surrender. about how he exited or completed the journey we are all on, the heroes journey (associated with becoming, or striving) and found a place of love in everything that allowed him a new form of radical acceptance.

this story was the grandest story I have ever heard so far, and it inspired me to look deeper than I have before. at the motives of people, of my own motives. my core drives. I found the categories of being and becoming to be more relevant than before, and I started constructing a framework around it. I constructed this story that every person that is living an examined life is on a search for a global minimum, and sometimes they hit a grove where they rapidly cycle through ever deeper local minima, and this is the heroes journey. I constructed this idea from observation of people in general, of myself, and of luv, that people all are agents of becoming, and at some point in their journey, at no fault of their own, they hit a local minimum that by some criteria is a sustainable place to build their home, and they switch from becoming to being.

.

I do not yet have a story about the structure of the meaning landscape, so I cannot yet say things like whether or not there is a global minimum, but I can say that when I told myself stories of being, and tried to replicate the path luv walked, and I saw landmarks of meaning that must have been a similar path walked by luv, I could not remain there. it was then that I understood for the first time which camp I am currently in. it seems to me that a part of me, a deep part of me, is still on the journey, is still searching, is still seeking a grand story. so I joined camp becoming. again, I do not know whether becoming or being is the mos prominent, or how they related, within the landscape of meaning, and depending on who you ask you will get different answers, but I know now that a fundamental part of me is still seeking. simply I don't know if becoming is objectively fundamental, but I know it's fundamental to me. and I hope to one day find out whether it should or should not be fundamental to me, so that I can be aligned with reality, and I hope this will be in the grand story

this is where I am right now, I am walking the meaning landscape and through telling story after story, trying to build the universe of being vs becoming in my own head. the ancient stories being told are incredibly helpful and I treat them just like I treat the stories I tell myself. some of them stick around longer than others, some of them make me sad, some of them make me ambitious, etc etc etc

it does seem to me that the Christian stories are deeper and more fundamental than usual, but there is still a lot of ground to cover and even after spending quite some time on it, they remain more vague than I would like them to be. I find myself telling stories that are a marriage between the Rationalist and Christian traditions that run through my heart. some things never change eh

Calling a movement an "aesthetic" acknowledges that a particular style can be executed "in good taste" or "in bad taste" without conflating ornamentation with implementation.

"Class" and "taste" can be discussed within the context of an aesthetic, but they cant define or impose an aesthetic a priori

For example, everyone likes to rag on brutalism these days, but brutalism can absolutely be executed in good taste. It just usually isnt. And art noveau, which is my preferred aesthetic, can absolutely be executed in poor taste.

A particular aesthetic mighr be easier or harder to implement in good or bad taste than another, but ultimately each work needs to be judged on its own merits.

But when you found a new movement, you arent founding a "taste"; you are founding an aesthetic and an art (which is a collection of techniques for implementing that aesthetic)

And a new aesthetic has to be allowed to experiment in bad taste, in order to discover what the good taste producing techniques are.

Exploring these pathways and seeing what survives is itself also very similar to my idea of the "tree of life" (and accordingly, the substance and signs of wisdom) being revealed within the "tree of knowledge of good and evil" (that which we construct by our own will). I think there is a lot of benefit to telling stories and seeing what we remember of them.

I find the stories in the Bible are distilled in ways that make them last better and adhere closer to reality. A good example is how in one of the most famous narratives, Moses opposing the pharaoh, the pharaoh is completely unnamed. Meanwhile, many lesser regional leaders *are* named. Scholars will differ on the significance here, but for me the degree to which the story allegorizes itself shows how foundational it is.

Jesus's parables are also the only example across the Old and New Testaments (with only some examples of storytelling happening in the Old Testament that are not about relaying dreams and visions and rather are about deliberate human authorial will) of someone repeatedly using stories for instruction and edification. And yet, history and scripture are themselves stories for instruction and edification; the New Testament is pretty much explicitly self-referential, while the Old Testament is only incidentally self-referential as a result of different texts within the canon having been written with others in mind.

Before I came to Christ, I told myself many stories that distilled my own experience, reducing different figures in my life into idealized characters that often combined many people or were composed of different parts of different people re-assembled like LEGO pieces. What ended up happening is that I wrote a sort of “Genesis” section to the story that I used to explain my life, which dug deeper into a completely unrelated and completely fictional mystery that I used in the telling to add dramatic flair. This ended up overtaking the rest of the story and becoming a narrative through which I meditated on psychology and archetypes and romantic love; now, it presents to me as a parable about teleology and the meaning of true worship vs. idolatry.

Every knee shall bow and every tongue shall confess that Jesus is Lord. This includes all stories. All stories must speak to the truth, with or without the intentions of its proximate author, or else perish. The narrative of *The Wind at Dawn* (the story of Gale Jones in Montreal in 1999 to which I referred in the paragraph above) was written at first by a God-hating reprobate, but it turned out to be the key to me seeking God.

One of my favorite advanced poker theory terms is "valor", the ability for a hand to be bet on a future street. There is a possible move called a valor sacrifice where you bet something more now at a larger than usual size which expends all your ability to bet the hand for value on a future street, but overall makes you more money than betting two streets.

And there are other basic ideas like "balanced range" where you have different classes of hand within a bet size to prevent them from putting you on exactly some class of hands and exploiting the heck out of you.

"The best of what is inevitable" is an interesting way to look at it because the best of what is inevitable  is still necessarily part of what is inevitable and so avoids following futility, but it focuses on the parts of God's plan to which we are more explicitly reconciled.

Related to perspective mapping, I've been intending to build a prototype for our civic hacknight where we have [live talks](https://guild.host/events/civic-meetup-527-designing-enx7le). You might also be interested in it if your audience for any of your video media is large enough... I tried to visually prototype it real quick:

this is the prototype app I built (it uses websockets to live swarm viewer, but you'll need to open another device to see it): https://polislike-partykit-reaction-canvas.patcon.partykit.dev/?room=3ntrtcehas
or I suppose this link fakes other participants: https://polislike-partykit-reaction-canvas.patcon.partykit.dev/?room=3ntrtcehas&ghostCursors=true
it's an extension of the premise that polislike tools engage with, but with video stimuli instead of statement stimuli (provoking reactions of away vs toward in meaning-space)

the idea is that you use the valence/reaction data to create a 2D map, then (with enough data) you can see the trajectories of how people transition between belief states. just like with gene expression matrices, which let you see (via gene expression patterns) transitions between healthy cell developmental states, and also cancer states

though i'm not making claims that I know what "cancer states" are in belief space.... but perhaps if we can all see the maps, we can better understand that (i also don't expect that scope of interest to be such an extreme for us, at least within the scope of your audience or mine 🙂 )

If you're down, I could create an MVP for some video, and we could invite ppl to watch it and react with their thumbs (ideally one that might be interestingly polarizing). the idea is that the interface is no-look. you just hold your thumb on it as you put full attention on something else (video on a monitor or a live speaker).

We'd need about 20 series of participant data to get something meaningful, I'd say

Oh hey, yes, this is akin to that, but with dimensional reduction over the whole set of interactions, and creating a map of participants from the patterns in reactions over time! Participant placement is a product of the whole set of their own interactions in relation to others' (one persons reactions to concepts will often be contradictory, but the product of their unique experience and path through spacetime, and perhaps contradictory like some subset of others' )

As in, if there are only 5 types of "sets of interactions", that kinda condenses down to 5 hotspots of participants on the perspective map, like an urban center in the "perspective space". And one can kinda reduce that mentally to "there are 5 general archetypes", but at the same time, also elevate and/or center intra-archetype perspectives as desired

And this all takes maybe 4 minutes to update if there are 30,000 participants and 1000 reactions. It's very scalable and quite instantaneous compared to other methods that try to seek feedback at scale
